title: Multi-horizon discrete hazard model instead of a single next-lap label
summary: Survival-stacking and discrete-time hazard literature suggest modeling conditional event risk over future intervals can be more stable than directly fitting only the one-step target.
rationale: Survival-stacking and discrete-time hazard literature suggest modeling conditional event risk over future intervals can be more stable than directly fitting only the one-step target. For pit stops, predicting hazards for the next 1, 2, 3, and 5 laps should regularize noisy boundary cases and encode time-to-stop structure.
implementation_hint: Create auxiliary labels for pit within <=k laps on uncensored driver-race prefixes, train a shared model with multi-output heads or separate models, then derive the final next-lap probability from the hazard head while using longer-horizon heads as auxiliary supervision. Enforce consistency so P(stop<=1) <= P(stop<=2) <= P(stop<=3) <= P(stop<=5).
expected_effect: Improves stability and recall around ambiguous windows where the car is close to pitting but not exactly on the next lap.
risk: If auxiliary heads dominate, the model may smooth away sharp next-lap signals; monitor AUC specifically on the original target and keep horizon labels causal.
